heading: MODULO DICIASSETTESIMO
card_summary: Chunk 134 treats the union of the blessed with God (Unione), drawing on John 17:11 and 17:21 (the High Priestly Prayer), Plotinus's Enneads on mystical vision, Augustine on the single Good, and the Beatitudes of Matthew 5 read as a graduated itinerary of purgation and deification. Zorzi argues that tending toward the One and actually becoming one with the One are two distinct acts, a secret reserved for those who have experienced union in deed or contemplation. Paul's "I live, yet not I, but Christ lives in me" (Gal 2:20) and his rapture to the third heaven (2 Cor 12:2-4) are cited as experiential testimony, alongside Pseudo-Dionysius's doctrine that love unites lover and beloved. Plato's Parmenides provides the metaphysical scaffold for the One as origin, container, and end of all things, and the concluding Beatitudes—poverty of spirit, peacemaking, persecution for justice—mark the final stages of stripping away multiplicity to achieve the eternal beatitude that is God himself.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 134 constitutes the single chapter entitled *Unione* ("On the Union of the Blessed with God") within the Seventeenth Module of the *Tono Ottavo* of the *Cantico Terzo* in Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. It is one of the most philosophically dense passages in the entire treatise, bringing together Neoplatonic mystical theology, Gospel exegesis, Patristic citation, and Pseudo-Dionysian erotics in service of a single proposition: that the soul's journey does not merely culminate in the *visio Dei* affirmed by scholastic theology, but reaches beyond vision to genuine ontological union with the divine One.

This chapter stands at the apex of Zorzi's entire cosmological and mystical architecture. Throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*, the author has constructed an elaborate metaphysical system tracing the emanation of all being from the divine One through intelligible and sensible hierarchies, and the consequent return (*epistrophe*) of all creatures through virtue, contemplation, and love. Yet the doctrine of union itself—the ultimate telos of this cosmic circulation—has been treated only obliquely in earlier modules. Here, in what is arguably the most esoteric section of the entire work, Zorzi brings to bear all his hermeneutical resources to articulate precisely what union *is*, how it differs from related states (vision, intellection, enjoyment), and what theological and mystical prerequisites condition its possibility. The result is a synthesis that is neither purely scholastic nor purely Neoplatonic, but rather a distinctively Christian Kabbalistic reconciliation of these traditions.

The chapter's placement at the conclusion of the Tono Ottavo, the eighth tone within the Cantico Terzo, is itself significant. The octave—as musical interval, number, and cosmic symbol—recurs throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi* as the symbol of perfect consonance, the point where a musical cycle completes itself and resolves into a higher octave. Zorzi's invocation of the "perfect diapason" (*diapason perfetto*) in this chapter is thus not merely metaphorical but constitutive of the work's overall cosmological claim: that union with the divine One is the consummate harmony toward which all multiplicity perpetually tends, the octave resolved, the circle completed.

---

## The Opening Scriptural Argument: John 17

Zorzi opens by grounding his mystical theology in the farewell prayer of Christ in John 17, which he treats as a disclosure of esoteric doctrine concealed from nearly all human minds:

> *"Padre santo, conservali nel tuo nome, quelli che tu mi hai dati, affinché siano uno, come noi"* (Gv 17,11)

and:

> *"affinché anch'essi siano una cosa sola in noi"* (Gv 17,21)

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, pp. 2715–2716 (Italian translation of the Latin text)</cite>

The key move is exegetical precision: Christ does not merely promise future union but specifies its *mode* — "as you, Father, are in me and I in you." This Trinitarian structure of mutual indwelling becomes the template for creaturely deification. The argument echoes Meister Eckhart and the Rhineland mystical tradition, though Zorzi's proximate source is Plotinian.

The Johannine High Priestly Prayer is the canonical locus classicus for mystical union in Christian theology. Yet Zorzi's reading emphasizes a feature often overlooked in scholastic treatments: the prayer reveals union not as an eschatological reward contingent upon external divine grant, but as a *present reality* of participatory indwelling that Christ announces as already constitutive of the relation between the Father and himself. The phrase "as we are one" (*sicut et nos unum sumus*) signals that the unity predicated of believers is of the same *kind* as the eternal unity of the Godhead itself. This is dangerous territory exegetically—prone to accusations of pantheism or antinomianism—and yet Zorzi advances it with full consciousness of its radicality. The Gospel, read mystically, discloses that the incarnate Word was the exemplar and agent of a deification available to all who attain the purity and love of which John 17 speaks.

This exegetical move is reinforced by Zorzi's reading of the entire Johannine corpus as a mystical manual. The prologue's *Logos* doctrine (Jn 1:1-3), the Bread of Life discourse (Jn 6), the Vine and Branches image (Jn 15), and the indwelling promise ("the Father and I will come and make our dwelling in you," Jn 14:23) all cohere in a single trajectory toward union. Yet the High Priestly Prayer is unique in making union an *explicit petition*—Christ prays the Father that believers *become* one as the Godhead is one. This transforms the mystical state from the achievement of individual ascetical effort into the *answer to Christ's intercession*. Union is thus positioned simultaneously as the crown of human aspiration and as a gift of divine condescension. Zorzi's entire chapter is structured around reconciling these two poles: the soul must *tend* toward the One with all its faculties, yet can only *become* one with the One through a seizure and indwelling that transcends effort.

---

## Plotinus and the Prohibition on Disclosure

Zorzi immediately turns to Plotinus, whom he identifies as "rivelatore eminentissimo della dottrina di Platone e anzi della sapienza divina di cui Platone si era imbevuto attingendo agli oracoli dei profeti" — a formulation that inscribes Plotinus within the *prisca theologia* as a transmitter of prophetic wisdom reaching back through Plato to the Hebrew prophets. The citation concerns the mystery-religion injunction against revealing divine things to the uninitiated:

> *"Questo è il senso della prescrizione contenuta nei misteri: vietarne la divulgazione agli uomini non iniziati né purificati. Fu proibito, inoltre, di rivelare il divino ad altri, se non a colui che ha già avuto l'esperienza della contemplazione."*

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, p. 2716, citing Plotinus, *Enneads* VI.9 (De Bono seu de Uno)</cite>

This passage establishes a hermeneutic of esoteric reserve that structures the entire chapter: the doctrine of union is among the *arcana reconditissima* not permissible to speak of except to those who have experienced it.

The citation, drawn from Plotinus's *De Bono* (the sixth Ennead, Tractate 9, often called the "Sixth Ennead"), addresses a foundational question: on what grounds does Plotinus himself dare to speak of the One and the mystical union with it, given that the highest mysteries have traditionally been guarded from the profane? Plotinus's answer, which Zorzi here reproduces, points to a difference in *disclosure conditions*: the mystery religion forbade *external speech* about the sacred to those unprepared, not because knowledge is purely mystical and ineffable, but because the ineffable is dangerous to those who encounter it unpurified. Zorzi applies this principle directly to the *De Harmonia Mundi* itself—his treatise is written under a similar reserve, addressed to those capable of receiving it, those who have been "purified" by philosophy and theology. Yet the Christianization is immediate: *purification* is not merely the Platonic katharsis but involves grace, the sacraments, the imitation of Christ, and the mortification of will that the Beatitudes require.

The strategic importance of this appeal to Plotinian reserve cannot be overstated. Zorzi is positioning his most controversial claim—that creatures can achieve actual union with the divine One—within an established philosophical and mystical tradition that is already familiar with this teaching. By invoking Plotinus's doctrine of mystical henosis (henosis being the technical term for union with the One in Neoplatonic terminology), Zorzi argues *a fortiori* that if pagan philosophers experienced this and defended its necessity, Christian theology should certainly not reject it as impossible. The polemic is implicit but clear: scholastic theologians who restrict beatitude to vision and intellection have fallen away from the deeper Platonic wisdom that both Plotinus and the Christian mystical tradition preserve. Plotinus becomes, in Zorzi's hands, a prophetic voice confirming the Gospel's most radical promise.

---

## The Defense of Parmenides Against Aristotle

An unexpected polemical digression rehabilitates Parmenides against Aristotelian critique. Zorzi, citing Simplicius's commentary on Aristotle's *Physics*, argues that Aristotle attacks the *words* of Parmenides rather than his *meaning*, because Parmenides was "speaking in divine terms" (*termini divini*) far beyond the scope of natural philosophy to which Aristotle confined himself. The metaphysical point is: all things proceeded from the One, and all things that tend to return to the One must strip themselves of the multiplicity belonging to imagination, dialectic, and sense — precisely the faculties on which Aristotle relied. This is why Aristotle never reached the One.

The structural logic of procession and return (*proodos* / *epistrophe*) is here entirely orthodox Proclean Neoplatonism, but Zorzi deploys it to defend a biblical monism: multiplicity is the fallen condition, unity is eschatological restoration.

This rehabilitation of Parmenides is crucial for understanding Zorzi's entire metaphysical project. In medieval philosophy, Parmenides had become something of a philosophical ogre—his doctrine of Being as singular, unchanging, and undifferentiated seemed to contradict not only Aristotle's logic and natural philosophy but also the Christian doctrine of creation and the fundamental distinction between Creator and creature. Yet Zorzi, following Proclus's reinterpretation of Parmenides in the *Platonic Theology*, argues that Parmenides's monism is *spiritually* rather than *ontologically* misguided: that is, Parmenides correctly intuited the absolute simplicity and indivisibility of the ultimate principle (the One), but expressed it in the language of physical being (*to on*) when he should have spoken of trans-being (*epekeina tes ousias*, to borrow Plotinus's phrasing borrowed from Plato's *Republic*).

The polemical target is the entire Aristotelian-scholastic synthesis. Aristotle's God is the First Mover, the Unmoved Mover, the pure actuality (*actus purus*) that perfects the cosmos through the universal desire for it. Yet this God remains fundamentally distant from creation, related to it only as object of love, never achieving the mystical union that Parmenides intuited and Plotinus systematized. By citing Simplicius against Aristotle, Zorzi is not merely defending an ancient philosopher but asserting that the Aristotelian system, for all its logical rigor, lacks the mystical penetration required to grasp the highest truths. The critique is Proclean in its architecture but radically Christian in its intent: if multiplicity is to be overcome, if creatures are genuinely to return to the One, they must transcend not merely sensation or imagination but the very structures of Aristotelian being and becoming.

---

## Martha and Mary: The Distraction of Multiplicity

The Lukan pericope of Martha and Mary (Lk 10:41-42) is read as an ontological parable: *"Martha turbaris erga plurima, porro unum est necessarium"* ("Martha, you are troubled about many things, but one thing is necessary"). The Italian commentary glosses the *unum necessarium* as "quell'Uno nel quale si raccolgono tutte le cose," reading it as identification of the evangelical *unum* with the Platonic One. This is a characteristic Zorzian move: the Gospel text is rendered philosophically adequate to Neoplatonism without being reduced to it.

Augustine's exclamation — "Omuncolo, perché vaghi qua e là dietro a tante cose? Cerca piuttosto l'unico Bene" — reinforces the Patristic warrant.

The Martha and Mary narrative, which appears only in Luke, is one of the most fraught passages in medieval mysticism precisely because it seemed to privilege contemplation (*vita contemplativa*) over active charity (*vita activa*). Martha's complaint that Mary sits at Jesus's feet while Martha serves alone elicits Jesus's response that Mary has "chosen the better part" (*melior pars*), which shall not be taken from her. In scholastic exegesis, this became license for defending the contemplative life against the criticisms of advocates for active ministry. Yet Zorzi reads the passage at a deeper level: Martha's "troubling" about *many things* (*plurima*) is not merely a psychological distraction but an ontological condition—the soul enmeshed in multiplicity, dispersed among finite goods, unable to achieve the simplicity and unity necessary for union with the transcendent One.

Mary's choice is thus not merely superior within the vita contemplativa but eschatologically normative: the soul must *choose* radical simplicity, must *renounce* the apparent necessity of external works (though not sin), and must attend solely to the "one thing needful." Zorzi identifies this *unum* explicitly with the Platonic One—not through an arbitrary allegory but through the recognition that both the Gospel and the Platonic tradition teach the same truth: the infinite diversification of the finite world, for all its beauty and order, is ultimately a distraction from the ultimate reality toward which the soul eternally tends. The identification is neither reductive nor foreign; rather, it exemplifies the *prisca theologia* principle that the deepest truths appear across different traditions because all wisdom flows from the same divine source.

Augustine's exclamation reinforces this cosmic anthropology. The Augustinian framework—"Why do you wander here and there after so many things? Seek rather the One Good"—transposes the Martha/Mary narrative into the register of the soul's eternal tendency toward unity. Augustine himself, in the *Confessions*, narrates his own journey from the dispersal of desire across temporal goods to the unitive vision of the eternal Good. Zorzi reads Augustine as a historical witness to the doctrine of mystical union, and the Martha/Mary pericope as the evangelical summons that catalyzed Augustine's own conversion toward that unity.

---

## The Critical Distinction: Tending Toward the One vs. Becoming One

The philosophical heart of the chapter is a distinction that Zorzi identifies as almost universally neglected:

> *"C'è tuttavia una grande differenza tra il cercare l'Uno o tendere a esso e diventare una sola cosa con esso. D'altra parte quasi tutti i sapienti affermano che è necessario vi sia l'Uno, al quale tutti devono tendere. Pochi, però, o nessuno ha spiegato come tendendo verso quell'Uno si possa diventare con esso la stessa cosa."*

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, pp. 2716–2717</cite>

The scholastic doctrine of beatitude (*visio et fruitio Dei*) is implicitly critiqued as insufficient: it affirms vision and enjoyment but stops short of union. Zorzi claims the fuller doctrine is available in the Gospel (Jn 17), in Plotinus, and in Pseudo-Dionysius.

This distinction—between *tendentia* (tending toward) and *unio* (becoming one with)—is methodologically the crux of the chapter. Thomas Aquinas had famously defined beatitude as the vision of God combined with the fruition or enjoyment of God (*beatitudo consistit in actu intellectus videntis Deum, cum affectione amantis*). This is an immense advance over earlier theologies that treated beatitude as mere visional knowledge; it introduces the affective dimension, the will's participation in the beatific state. Yet Zorzi perceives an aporia in the scholastic formulation: even in the beatific vision, the soul remains *se* (itself), contemplating and loving God as an *alter* (other). The soul is perfected, transformed, elevated to participate in divine life, yet the fundamental duality of knower and known, lover and beloved, remains structurally intact. Vision and fruition, however exalted, are still *relational* acts presupposing two terms.

Union, by contrast, is the *dissolution of duality*. To become "one thing with the One" is to enter a state in which the distinction between subject and object, between the one who seeks and the One sought, collapses. This is precisely what Zorzi finds in Plotinus's henosis and what he identifies in Paul's rapture and Christ's High Priestly Prayer. The distinction he articulates here is not novel—it echoes the medieval distinction between *unio simplex* (simple union, in which duality persists but is overcome by grace) and *unio per transformationem* (union through transformation, in which the creature is wholly transfigured). Yet by grounding this distinction textually in Scripture and Plotinus simultaneously, Zorzi is making a polemical claim: the highest mystical experience testified to by the tradition is not merely contemplative vision but actual ontological union.

The hermeneutical significance of this distinction is that it allows Zorzi to defend the possibility and desirability of union against scholastic objections. If one asks, "Does the soul lose its identity in union?", the scholastic is inclined to say *no*, and therefore to deny that genuine union occurs—the soul remains itself, therefore it cannot truly become one with the Other. But Zorzi's distinction cuts differently: the soul does *lose* its separate identity in the act of union itself (*in ipsa unione*), yet paradoxically it does not cease to exist; rather, it exists more fully than ever, now pervaded and constituted by the divine presence. The logical scandal is intentional: union transcends the categories of identity and difference, presence and absence, self and other. This is why it can only be disclosed to those who have experienced it; it exceeds rational articulation.

---

## The Plotinian Phenomenology of Union

The longest single quotation in the chapter is from Plotinus (*Enneads* VI.9.11), rendered in the Latin of the Ficinian translation and then paraphrased in Italian. It describes the state of the one who has achieved union:

> *"Est enim ipse videns cum eo quod videtur, non duo, sed unum, tamquam non esset proprie visum aliquid, sed unitum... Non enim apud eum, qui sic videt, aliquid movetur, non ira, non appetitus alicuius inferioris... Sed tamquam raptus, sive a Deo penitus occupatus, in tuto quodam secessu, habituque tranquillo, prorsusque immobili iam constitutus est."*

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, p. 2717, citing Plotinus, *Enneads* VI.9.11 (Ficino's Latin translation)</cite>

The state described is one of perfect stillness: no movement, no anger, no inferior desire, no reason, no intellect, not even the self (*neque omnino ipsemet*). The mystic is "raptus" — seized — by God. Zorzi immediately identifies this condition with Paul's *raptus* (2 Cor 12:2-4) and with Gal 2:20 ("Vivo ego iam non ego, vivit vero in me Christus"), establishing Paul as the scriptural witness to an experience Plotinus describes from within the Neoplatonic tradition.

This passage from Plotinus constitutes the phenomenological core of Zorzi's doctrine of union. The Plotinian account is remarkably precise about what union *is not*: it is not vision (though vision precedes and occasions it); it is not intellection (though intellect must be transcended to achieve it); it is not emotion or affect (though love is the vehicle that transports the soul into union). Rather, union is a state of absolute simplicity and stillness, a *stasis* beyond all becoming, a condition in which the usual faculties of the soul—reason, intellect, sensation, emotion—are all "stilled," not annihilated but subsumed into a unity that is prior to their operations.

The key phrase—"the one who sees becomes one with what is seen, not two but one, as if there were not properly anything seen but something united"—articulates the collapse of subject-object duality. In ordinary vision, seer and seen remain distinct; the seen is the *object* of vision, present to the subject. Yet in mystical union with the One, this structure is transcended. There is no longer a *visum* (something seen) distinct from the *videns* (the seer), but rather a *unitum* (something united), a single reality that encompasses both. Zorzi recognizes in this formulation the exact phenomenology recorded in Christian mystical accounts: the dissolution of the boundary between subject and object, the paradoxical union-without-confusion that characterizes henosis.

The invocation of Paul's rapture ("raptus sive a Deo penitus occupatus") is a masterstroke of Christian hermeneutics. Paul's account in 2 Corinthians 12:2-4 describes being "caught up to the third heaven" and hearing "inexpressible things" (*arrhetoi logoi*), things "not permitted to tell." Zorzi reads this as Paul's attestation to the same henotic experience that Plotinus describes philosophically. The "inexpressibility" (*arrhesia*) is not due to Paul's lack of eloquence but to the nature of the experience itself: it transcends the categories of language, concepts, and rational articulation. Similarly, Gal 2:20 ("I no longer live, but Christ lives in me") describes a death of the empirical ego and its replacement by Christ—a perfect expression of the Plotinian paradox that in union, the self is not lost but transfigured and indwelt by the divine.

Crucially, Zorzi emphasizes that this state involves the cessation of all *movement* (*motus*), all *appetite* (*appetitus*), and even the operations of reason and intellect. This is significant because it distinguishes union from the contemplative vision of the One that Plotinus elsewhere describes as the culmination of the soul's ascent through the intelligible realm. In those passages, the soul contemplates the divine beauty, the infinite ideation of the One, the intelligible cosmos. Yet henosis proper goes beyond this: it is a condition prior to or beyond contemplation, a state in which even the most exalted intellective activity is stilled in favor of a *formless* unity with the utterly transcendent source of all being and intelligibility. Zorzi's insistence on this distinction between contemplative vision and henotic union is essential to his entire mystical theology, because it guards against the reduction of union to mere intellectual achievement. No ascetical effort, no amount of philosophical rigor, can produce henosis; it is pure grace, pure gift, pure divine seizure.

---

## Pseudo-Dionysius and the Erotic Union

Moving from vision to love, Zorzi introduces Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite to argue that eros is a stronger vehicle of union than intellect:

> *"si colui che ha la visione divina diventa una cosa sola con l'oggetto della visione (come aveva detto Plotino)... ancor più e in modo più efficace l'amante diventa una cosa sola con l'amato... Dionigi e moltissimi altri sapienti affermano che l'amore è la virtù che unisce l'amante con l'amato."*

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, p. 2718, citing Pseudo-Dionysius, *De Divinis Nominibus* IV</cite>

This introduces the Dionysian *eros* as unitive force, a theme Zorzi has developed throughout the *Cantico Terzo*. The speculative and contemplative faculties strip away multiplicity in order to contemplate the One; so too the operative and affective faculties must proceed from many back to One. This convergence — intellectual and volitional — "compie il diapason perfetto e la compiuta armonia," the perfect octave and consummated harmony that gives the whole treatise its title.

Pseudo-Dionysius had revolutionized medieval mystical theology by rehabilitating the term *eros* (divine love, erotic love) as not merely permissible but essential to mystical theology. Working within the Neoplatonic framework inherited from his source Proclus, Pseudo-Dionysius argued that the cosmic procession of all beings from the divine source and their return to it are moved and effected by *eros*—by the transcendent desire that pulls all things toward their origin and source. This divine *eros* is not passion or privation (as Aristotle had argued) but rather the fundamental creative and unitive power of God. In *De Divinis Nominibus* IV, Pseudo-Dionysius describes eros as the force that binds lover and beloved, that effects union across the distinction of subject and object.

Zorzi's deployment of Pseudo-Dionysius at this juncture serves multiple functions. First, it validates the affective and erotic dimension of the mystical ascent against any purely intellectualist reduction. Second, it establishes a hierarchy of unitive powers: vision unites the soul to its object, but erotic love unites with greater efficacy and immediacy. This is profoundly Christian, because it privileges love (*agape*, *caritas*) over knowledge, following the Pauline dictum that "now we see through a glass darkly, but then face to face" (1 Cor 13:12) and the insistence that "love never fails" even when prophecy and knowledge cease. Third, it allows Zorzi to integrate both the contemplative and affective traditions within a single framework: the via contemplativa and the via affectiva are not competitors but complementary approaches to the same telos, union with God.

The invocation of the "perfect diapason and consummated harmony" at this juncture is programmatic. The diapason—the octave, the interval from a note to its double—was understood in medieval music theory as the most perfect of all consonances, the point at which a melodic or harmonic series resolves and completes itself before ascending to a higher octave. Zorzi employs this musical metaphor to describe the union of the intellectual and affective dimensions of the soul's ascent. Just as the diapason requires both the lower and upper octaves, sounding together in perfect consonance, so too the mystical union requires both the contemplative stripping away of multiplicity and the erotic movement of the will toward the beloved One. Neither alone suffices; only together do they produce the "perfect consonance" (*consonantia perfecta*) that characterizes henosis. This musical language is not merely decorative; it reflects Zorzi's deep conviction that music, harmony, and proportion are the very fabric of reality, from the cosmic order down to the microcosmic soul. Union with the One is the ultimate harmony, the point at which all discord is resolved and all striving ceases in perfect rest.

---

## Plato's Parmenides and the Metaphysics of the One

Zorzi invokes Plato's *Parmenides* directly to characterize the One:

> *"Esse si sforzano di raggiungere quell'Uno che è (come insegna Platone nel Parmenide) ciò dal quale tutto proviene, è al di fuori di tutte le cose e, allo stesso tempo, è in tutte, ed è ciò cui tutte tendono, perché sia il primo e l'ultimo, il principio e la fine, come attestano in molti passi le sacre Scritture."*

<cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, pp. 2718–2719, citing Plato, *Parmenides* and Apocalypse 1:8 / 22:13</cite>

The characterization — "a quo omnia, extra omnia, et in omnibus, et ad quod omnia tendunt" — is a standard Proclean formula for the First Principle, here harmonized with the Johannine *Alpha and Omega*. The argument that creatures will necessarily reach the One because an appetite cannot be planted in nature *in vain* (*ne frustra appetitus ille insitus sit*) is a Thomistic-Aristotelian teleological argument redirected toward mystical universalism: all things will return to their source.

Critically, Zorzi insists that creatures will not cease to be what they are upon union: they will be "trasformate in meglio" and "vivranno più felicemente" — a safeguard against pantheistic absorption, maintaining creaturely distinctness within union.

The Platonic *Parmenides* is one of the most enigmatic dialogues in the Platonic corpus, and medieval and Renaissance philosophers engaged in fierce debate over its correct interpretation. Zorzi here relies on the Proclean reading, which interprets the first hypothesis of the dialogue (the One beyond being) as a description of the transcendent first principle from which all multiplicity emanates. The characterization Zorzi offers—"from which all things come forth, outside all things yet in all things, toward which all things tend"—captures the paradoxical ontological status of the One in Neoplatonic metaphysics. The One is *epekeina tes ousias* (beyond being), transcendent and utterly simple, and yet it is the source and ground of all reality, the immanent principle sustaining all things in existence. This paradox—that the most transcendent is also the most immanent, that the utterly simple is the source of infinite complexity—stands at the heart of Neoplatonic theology and, Zorzi argues, at the heart of Christian theology as well.

The fusion of Plato's One with the Johannine Alpha and Omega is quintessentially Zorzian. Apocalypse 1:8 ("I am the Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end") and 22:13 (repeated verbatim) assert that the divine principle is simultaneously the *beginning* and *end* of all things, their *arche* and *telos*. This harmonizes perfectly with the Neoplatonic doctrine of procession and return: all things proceed from the One as their source and all things return to the One as their end. Yet the Apocalyptic language adds an eschatological and Christological dimension absent from abstract Neoplatonism: the One who is Alpha and Omega is the risen Christ, the Logos enfleshed, the Word through whom all things were made and toward whom all things are drawn. Zorzi's synthesis suggests that Plato intuited the structure of the cosmic order (procession and return, emanation and reversion) while remaining ignorant of its personal ground in Christ.

The teleological argument that Zorzi invokes—that an appetite planted in nature cannot be in vain, and therefore all creatures must necessarily attain their desired end—is drawn from Aristotelian natural philosophy as filtered through Thomas Aquinas. Yet Zorzi deploys it in service of a claim far more radical than scholastic theology typically ventures: not merely that all creatures *can* return to the divine source, or that those who freely choose to do so *will* achieve beatitude, but that all creatures *must* eventually return, that universal restoration is metaphysically inevitable. This touches on the question of *apokatastasis* (universal restoration or recapitulation of all things in Christ) that haunted medieval theology as a dangerous but persistent hope. Zorzi does not explicitly invoke the term, but the logic of his teleological argument points in that direction: if the appetite for the One is inscribed in the very nature of creatures, and if this appetite cannot be vain or frustrated, then all creatures must eventually satisfy it.

Yet Zorzi immediately guards against pantheism by insisting that creatures, in achieving union, do not lose their *quid* (whatness), their fundamental creaturely nature. They are "transformed for the better" (*trasformate in meglio*) and "will live more happily" (*vivranno più felicemente*), but they do not cease to be themselves or merge undifferentiatedly into the divine essence. This distinction—between henosis and pantheistic absorption—is subtle but crucial. The One remains truly other, transcendent, utterly simple; yet creatures in union with it are simultaneously most fully themselves, perfected according to their proper nature and capacity. This is the mysterious doctrine of theosis or deification: the creature becomes as much as possible like God, participates in divine life, is indwelt by divine presence, and yet remains creature. The distinction of Creator and creature is preserved even within the closest possible union.

---

## The Beatitudes as Itinerary of Mystical Ascent

The final section reads the Beatitudes of Matthew 5 as a systematic itinerary of purification leading to union. Zorzi follows a logic of progressive stripping:

1. **Purity of heart** (Mt 5:8: *Beati mundo corde, quoniam ipsi Deum videbunt*) — required for vision and contemplation of the One to whom we must be united.
2. **Poverty of spirit** (Mt 5:3: *Beati pauperes spiritu*) — total non-attachment to exterior goods; the kingdom of heaven is identified with God himself.
3. **Peacemaking** (Mt 5:9: *Beati pacifici, quoniam filii Dei vocabuntur*) — linked to John 1:12 ("dedit eis potestatem filios Dei fieri") and interpreted as the fruit of having mastered the animal dimension of the soul.
4. **Persecution for justice** (Mt 5:10-12) — the final stage, reserved for those who have become "inconcussi" (unshakeable), dead to externals, raised above the human condition. Their reward (*merces*) is God himself, who said "Ego ero merces tua magna nimis" (Gen 15:1).

The terminal Beatitude closes the chapter with the identification of the eschatological reward with the divine One: God "si diffonde con abbondanza e unisce più strettamente a sé gli amanti nella beatitudine e nella felicità eterna."

The Beatitudes occupy a unique place in Zorzi's theological architecture. They are not merely moral injunctions or consolations for the suffering righteous; they are metaphysical and mystical principles describing the transformative journey of the soul from its condition of wretchedness and multiplicity toward union with the divine simplicity. The logic of Zorzi's reading is progressive and ascending: each Beatitude presupposes and builds upon the previous, each involves a deeper stripping away of attachments and illusions, each moves the soul closer to the radical poverty and nakedness required for mystical union.

The first Beatitude Zorzi treats—purity of heart—is presented as the prerequisite for vision. This follows the Johannine principle that "blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God" (*Beati mundo corde, quoniam ipsi Deum videbunt*, Mt 5:8). Purity (*puritas cordis*) is understood as the purification of the intellective faculty from all phantasms, passions, and imaginative content, the *catharsis* that enables the soul to contemplate intelligible realities without the interference of sensible desire. Yet Zorzi notes that vision alone does not suffice for union; one can contemplate and remain distinct from the object of contemplation. The soul must progress beyond purity of vision to poverty of spirit.

Poverty of spirit (*paupertas spiritus*, Mt 5:3) is the second stage and perhaps the most essential to Zorzi's entire mystical theology. The "poor in spirit" are those who have surrendered all attachments to external goods—wealth, honor, power, even the goods of the body and imagination. Yet more radically, "poverty of spirit" involves a stripping away of the very sense of the separate self, the ego that asserts itself in pride and possessiveness. The Gospel promises such souls "the kingdom of heaven" (*basileia tou ouraniou*), which Zorzi identifies not with a place or future realm but with God himself, the transcendent One who is the true "kingdom" (*basileia*) toward which all things are ordered. To be poor in spirit is to have nothing—no attachments, no separate will, no ego-sense—and therefore to be entirely open and receptive to the divine fullness.

Peacemaking (*eirenepoioi*, Mt 5:9) is the third stage, linked by Zorzi to the acquisition of inner peace and harmony. Through the mortification of exterior passions and interior attachments, the soul achieves a state of *irenic* rest, a peace that passeth understanding. This peace is not mere psychological tranquility but a reordering of the entire person, a harmonization of the conflicting desires and wills within the soul. The Gospel promises that the peacemakers "shall be called children of God" (*huioi theou klethesontai*), which Zorzi connects to John 1:12: to all who receive the Word, "he gave power to become children of God" (*exousia tekna theou genesthai*). The implication is that peacemaking—the achievement of inner harmony through the mortification of multiplicity—constitutes the condition for divine adoption, for becoming *truly* God's child through participation in the life of the divine Son.

The final Beatitude—persecution for righteousness (*Mt* 5:10-12)—is reserved for those who have advanced to the highest stages of the mystical way. Those who are persecuted "for righteousness' sake" are those who have become wholly identified with divine justice, so thoroughly stripped of personal will and attachment that they are unmoved by external opposition and suffering. Zorzi describes them as "inconcussi"—unmoved, unshakeable—because they have risen above the human condition of fear and desire. Their reward (*merces*) is identified with God himself, quoting Abraham's vision in Genesis 15:1: "I am your very great reward" (*Ego ero merces tua magna nimis*). The union with God that is the fruit of martyrdom is not a future compensation but the very being and presence of God indwelling the soul, more intimate and essential than any external blessing.

The cumulative movement of the Beatitudes traces a path of systematic deification. Beginning with the purification necessary for contemplation, proceeding through the radical dispossession of poverty of spirit, advancing through the harmonization of peacemaking, and culminating in the martyrdom of those wholly given over to divine justice, the Beatitudes form a graduated ascent toward henosis. At each stage, something is surrendered—attachments, sense of separate self, peace based on external circumstances, the very claim to personal right—and something is received in exchange: vision, the kingdom, divine sonship, intimate union with God. Yet Zorzi emphasizes that this is not a purely human achievement; it is a cooperative process in which grace—the divine gift and indwelling—both motivates and sustains the soul's ascent. The Beatitudes thus synthesize the gospel's moral teaching, the mystical theology of union, and the doctrine of theosis into a single comprehensive path of transformation.

---

## Structural and Theological Significance

This chapter represents Zorzi's most sustained attempt to go beyond the scholastic doctrine of beatitude (*visio et fruitio Dei*) toward a mystical doctrine of ontological union grounded simultaneously in the Fourth Gospel, Plotinus's *De Bono* (Enn. VI.9), Pseudo-Dionysius's *De Divinis Nominibus*, Platonic metaphysics, and the Sermon on the Mount. The hermeneutical method is characteristic of Christian Kabbalah in the Pico–Reuchlin–Zorzi lineage: convergence of *prisca theologia* sources as mutual illumination rather than reduction of one to another. The "perfect diapason" and "consummated harmony" of the title are explicitly invoked at the structural midpoint of the chapter to signal that this mystical union is the telos toward which the entire cosmological architecture of the *De Harmonia Mundi* has been oriented.

The theological and metaphysical claims advanced in this chapter are among the most daring in Renaissance theology. By insisting on actual henotic union as the culmination of the spiritual way, Zorzi ventures beyond the boundaries of orthodox scholasticism into territory that had been contested and even condemned. The doctrine of union with God had been defended by medieval mystics—Eckhart most famously, though controversially—and by the Christian Neoplatonists of the Florentine Academy. Yet to systematize it, to ground it in authoritative texts from Scripture, Platonic philosophy, Patristic theology, and the Jewish mystical tradition, and to present it as the telos of all created being, was an extraordinary intellectual risk. Zorzi takes this risk because he perceives in the convergence of these traditions a deeper truth that scholasticism, for all its logical rigor, had obscured: that the human soul is radically ordered toward deification, that creation itself is the expression of divine love drawing all things back to their source, and that union with God is not a supernumerary grace or theological speculation but the very heart of the Gospel message and the destiny written into the structure of being itself.
